nodes.text: Good evening. The day is done — almost. Before you close it, take five minutes to understand it. No judgement. No scoring. Just honest reflection.
If today were a weather report, what would it be?
options: Sunny — things clicked
Cloudy — low energy, low output
Stormy — friction everywhere
Mixed — patches of both
answer=Sunny — things clicked|Mixed — patches of both:A1_Q_AGENCY_HIGH
answer=Cloudy — low energy, low output|Stormy — friction everywhere:A1_Q_AGENCY_LOW

nodes.text: You said "{A1_OPEN.answer}". When something went well today, what drove it?
options: I prepared for it
I adapted when things shifted
The team came through
Luck — right place, right time

nodes.text: You said "{A1_OPEN.answer}". When things got hard, where did your mind go first?
options: What I could still control
What others should have done differently
How unfair the situation was
Whether to push through or give up
answer=I prepared for it|I adapted when things shifted:A1_Q2_INTERNAL
answer=The team came through|Luck — right place, right time:A1_Q2_MIXED
answer=What I could still control|Whether to push through or give up:A1_Q2_MIXED
answer=What others should have done differently|How unfair the situation was:A1_Q2_EXTERNAL

nodes.text: Think of one moment today where something didn't go as planned. What did you do?
options: Changed my approach on the spot
Decided it wasn't worth my energy
Asked for help and kept moving
Sat with it longer than I should have

nodes.text: Was there a moment today where you had more control than you initially thought?
options: Yes — I realized I could adjust something
Maybe — I didn't think about it that way
Not really — the constraints were real
I'm not sure

nodes.text: If you could replay one interaction from today, what would you change about your own response?
options: I'd speak up sooner
I'd stay calmer
I'd ask more questions instead of assuming
Honestly, nothing — the other person was the problem
signal=axis1:internal>=2:A1_R_INT
signal=axis1:internal<2:A1_R_EXT

nodes.text: You stayed in the driver's seat today — even when the road was rough. That's not optimism. That's a skill. Agency isn't about controlling outcomes; it's about not outsourcing your response to circumstances.

Hard days pull attention outward — to what others did, what luck dealt, what wasn't fair. That's human. But somewhere today, you made a call. Even 'doing nothing' was a choice. That's where your agency lives.
You've thought about how you handled today. Now let's look at what you gave.
Think about your energy today — where did most of it go?
options: Into doing my work well
Into helping someone else
Into protecting myself from wasted effort
Into wondering if I was being recognized
answer=Into doing my work well|Into helping someone else:A2_Q_CONTRIBUTION
answer=Into protecting myself from wasted effort|Into wondering if I was being recognized:A2_Q_ENTITLEMENT

nodes.text: Was there a moment today where you gave something — time, knowledge, effort — without expecting it back?
options: Yes, and it felt natural
Yes, but I noticed I wanted credit for it
No — I was running on empty myself
I don't think I had the opportunity

nodes.text: When you think about what you deserved today — recognition, resources, support — how does it feel compared to what you got?
options: I got more than I expected
About right — fair exchange
Less than I put in
Significantly less — I was overlooked
answer=Yes, and it felt natural:A2_Q2_GIVE
answer=Yes, but I noticed I wanted credit for it|No — I was running on empty myself|I don't think I had the opportunity:A2_Q2_MIXED
answer=I got more than I expected|About right — fair exchange:A2_Q2_MIXED
answer=Less than I put in|Significantly less — I was overlooked:A2_Q2_OWED

nodes.text: What's something you could give tomorrow — one specific thing — that nobody asked you for?
options: Feedback that would actually help someone
Taking something off a colleague's plate
Making a process clearer for the next person
Nothing comes to mind right now

nodes.text: If you had to be honest: today, were you more focused on what you owed the team, or what the team owed you?
options: What I owed — I was thinking about my contribution
What they owed — I was keeping score
Both, switching back and forth
Neither — I was just trying to get through the day

nodes.text: The feeling of being owed is real. But what did you give today — even small — that nobody might notice?
options: I showed up and did the work, even when it was hard
I kept a difficult feeling to myself so others weren't affected
I stayed later than I needed to
Honestly, I'm not sure I gave much today
signal=axis2:contribution>=2:A2_R_CONT
signal=axis2:contribution<2:A2_R_ENT

nodes.text: You were oriented toward giving today. That's not self-sacrifice — it's the most durable source of professional meaning. People who focus on contribution don't wait to feel motivated. They act, and the meaning follows.

Noticing what you deserve is human — especially after effort that went unrecognized. But entitlement, when it runs quietly in the background, converts work into ledger entries. What would happen if you stopped counting for one day?
You've reflected on how you acted and what you gave. Now, one last shift — how wide was your world today?
When you think about today's biggest challenge, who comes to mind?
options: Just me — it was mine to deal with
My immediate team — we were all in it
A specific person who was struggling more than me
The people we're ultimately serving — customers, users, the mission
answer=Just me — it was mine to deal with:A3_Q_SELF
answer=My immediate team — we were all in it:A3_Q_TEAM
answer=A specific person who was struggling more than me|The people we're ultimately serving — customers, users, the mission:A3_Q_OTHER

nodes.text: Was there anyone today whose day was affected by how you showed up — positively or negatively?
options: Yes — someone was directly impacted by my work
Probably, but I didn't track it
I'm not sure my work touched anyone else today
I deliberately kept to myself today

nodes.text: In a team situation, did you think about the group's need or mostly about your part in it?
options: The group's outcome mattered more than my contribution
I did my part and trusted the team with theirs
I was more worried about my piece than the whole
I lost sight of the team in the stress of the day

nodes.text: When you thought about that person or those people — did it make your own challenge feel smaller, larger, or just different?
options: Smaller — it gave me perspective
Larger — I felt more responsible
Different — it reframed the problem
I thought about it briefly but came back to my own stress
signal=axis3:other>=1:A3_Q2_WIDE
signal=axis3:other<1:A3_Q2_NARROW

nodes.text: When you contribute to something beyond yourself, how does that typically affect your own experience of the work?
options: It makes difficult days more bearable
It's energizing — I work better for it
It's meaningful but also draining
I've never really thought about it that way

nodes.text: Is there someone — a colleague, a user, anyone — whose experience of today you don't know yet but could check on?
options: Yes — and I probably should
Maybe — I can think of someone
Not really — my work is pretty independent
I hadn't thought about anyone else's day
signal=axis3:other>=2:A3_R_WIDE
signal=axis3:other<2:A3_R_NARROW

nodes.text: Your radius extended beyond yourself today. Maslow called this self-transcendence — not self-sacrifice, but the shift from 'what do I need?' to 'what does the situation need from me?' That shift is the difference between enduring work and finding it meaningful.

Today your world was mostly your own. That's not a flaw — some days demand inward focus just to survive. But notice: most suffering at work shrinks when we look outward at someone else's challenge. You don't have to stop mattering to yourself. You just have to start mattering to someone else too.

axis1.internal: You owned your responses today — that's the foundation everything else builds on.
axis1.external: Tomorrow, watch for one small moment where you had more control than you first thought.

axis2.contribution: You gave today. That's a practice, not a personality — keep practicing.
axis2.entitlement: The ledger can wait. What if tomorrow you gave one thing and didn't track whether it came back?

axis3.other: Your concern extended outward today. That's where the deepest meaning in work lives.
axis3.self: Tomorrow, look up once. Notice someone else's challenge. You don't have to fix it — just see it.

closingInsights.internal_contribution_other: Agency. Giving. Perspective. Today had all three. That's a good day — even if it didn't feel like one.
closingInsights.internal_contribution_self: You acted well and gave well. Widen the circle tomorrow.
closingInsights.internal_entitlement_other: You saw the bigger picture — use that clarity to stop keeping score.
closingInsights.internal_entitlement_self: You had agency today. Now practice using it in service of something beyond yourself.
closingInsights.external_contribution_other: You gave without always feeling in control. That's real generosity.
closingInsights.external_contribution_self: You gave today. Tomorrow, see if doing that for someone specific changes how the day feels.
closingInsights.external_entitlement_other: Hard day. But you still looked outward. Hold onto that instinct.
closingInsights.external_entitlement_self: Tomorrow is a reset. One moment of control. One thing given. One look outward. Start there.

nodes.text: See you tomorrow.
